title: Estimating Bad Debts — % of Sales vs Aging
event: Reyes Co. estimates uncollectibles at year-end. Credit sales were $400,000, and the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts has an existing credit balance of $1,200. Prepare the adjusting entry.

lines.label: 400,000 × 0.5%
lines.why: The sales method computes the EXPENSE directly — matched to the revenue that created the risk.
lines.trap: The existing 1,200 balance is IGNORED under this method — netting it here mixes the two approaches.

lines.label: cushion grows
lines.why: The contra-asset builds toward future write-offs.
lines.trap: Never credit AR — no specific customer has failed yet.

lines.label: 5,500 − 1,200
lines.why: Aging sets the required ENDING balance; the entry plugs the gap.
lines.trap: Using the full 5,500 double-counts the cushion that already exists.

lines.label: topped up to 5,500
lines.why: Allowance lands exactly at the aging's requirement.
lines.trap: If the existing balance were a DEBIT, you'd ADD it instead: 5,500 + 1,200.

memorize.body: % of sales → EXPENSE directly (ignore existing allowance). Aging → ending BALANCE (net the existing allowance).
Net realizable value = gross AR − allowance. That's the number the balance sheet actually reports.

questions.prompt: Aging requires a $5,500 ending allowance; the account holds a $1,200 credit. Bad debt expense = ?

distractors.feedback: That's the required ENDING balance — part of it already exists. The entry adds only the 4,300 gap.
You ADDED the existing credit balance. Credits get subtracted from the target: 5,500 − 1,200.

build.scaffold: Method first: sales % → compute expense directly; aging → target minus what's already there.
